medicare_employer_working.title: Your Job Insurance Pays First
medicare_employer_working.summary: Good news! Since you or your spouse still works, your job insurance pays first. This means you CAN use copay cards to lower your costs.
tips: Show your job insurance card at the pharmacy first
You can use copay cards from drug makers
Keep your Medicare card as backup

medicare_retiree.title: Medicare Pays First
medicare_retiree.summary: Since you retired, Medicare now pays first. You cannot use copay cards with Medicare. But you can still get help from Patient Assistance Programs.
tips: Apply for Patient Assistance Programs (PAPs) for free meds
Check if foundations can help with your costs
Your retiree plan helps pay what Medicare does not

medicare_medicaid.title: You Have Great Coverage
medicare_medicaid.summary: You have dual coverage. This usually means very low or no costs for your medicines. Medicaid helps pay what Medicare does not cover.
tips: Your copays should be very low or $0
Make sure both cards are on file at your pharmacy
Ask about "Extra Help" to lower costs even more

esrd_employer.title: Special Rules for Kidney Patients
esrd_employer.summary: Kidney patients have special rules. For the first 30 months, your job insurance pays first. After 30 months, Medicare pays first and you cannot use copay cards.
tips: Use copay cards now while your job insurance pays first
Mark your calendar for when 30 months ends
Plan ahead: apply for PAPs before Medicare takes over

not_sure.title: Let's Figure This Out
not_sure.summary: Insurance rules can be tricky. The best way to know for sure is to call both insurance companies and ask: "Which plan pays first?"
tips: Ask your transplant social worker for help

start.title: Do You Have Two Insurance Plans?
start.subtitle: Many transplant patients have more than one insurance. This quiz helps you know which one pays first.
start.whyText: Which insurance pays first changes what help you can get. Some savings programs only work with certain insurance types.
start.reasonsTitle: Common reasons transplant patients have two plans:

reasons.kidney: Kidney disease: You may get Medicare even if you're under 65
reasons.disability: Disability: After 2 years on disability, you get Medicare
reasons.working: Still working at 65+: You have Medicare AND job insurance
reasons.spouse: Spouse's insurance: Your spouse's job covers you too

question1.title: Do you have more than one health insurance plan?
question1.subtitle: For example: Medicare AND insurance from a job
question1.yes: Yes, I have two or more plans
question1.no: No, I have just one plan

question2.title: Which best describes your situation?
question2.subtitle: Pick the one that fits you best

medicare_employer_working.label: I have Medicare AND job insurance
medicare_employer_working.detail: I or my spouse still works

medicare_retiree.label: I have Medicare AND retiree insurance
medicare_retiree.detail: I'm retired but still have coverage from my old job

esrd_employer.label: I have kidney disease Medicare AND job insurance
esrd_employer.detail: I got Medicare because of kidney failure (ESRD)

result.onePlanText: Since you have just one plan, you don't need to worry about which pays first. Use our main quiz to find help programs for your situation.
result.papYes: Yes, you can apply for PAPs

page.title: Frequently Asked Questions
page.subtitle: Find answers to common questions about transplant medications, assistance programs, and using this site.
page.emptyText: Check back later for frequently asked questions.
page.stillText: Your transplant center's social worker or financial coordinator is your best resource for personalized guidance.
